patches.rationale: Chapman (1933) was published in Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38(3), not Phil. Trans. Royal Society A. The doi 10.1098/rsta.1933.0001 resolves to a different paper. Wrong DOI on our foundational citation would allow dome defenders to claim fabrication. Replace with plain text citation of the correct journal.

patches.find: The dome's own 'honest caveat' concedes that 'Globe has a competing ionospheric conductivity mechanism' and that 'Tier 1 is consistent with both models.' This is an admission that WIN-068 is non-discriminating — it does not distinguish the dome from standard physics. The dome then claims the discriminating test is 'contact-phase timing (C1/C4, not totality peak),' but Kim & Chang's data actually show maximum effect near eclipse maximum, not at contact phases — directly contradicting the dome's claimed discriminator.
patches.replace: The dome's Steelman Evidence Ladder places the eclipse geomagnetic observation at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models — an admission that WIN-068 alone cannot distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics. The dome proposes that contact-phase timing (electromagnetic response at first/last contact vs. totality peak) would be discriminating for a future test. But Kim & Chang's data show the maximum effect over a ~180-minute window centered on eclipse maximum — consistent with the Sq current model's gradual response as the shadow sweeps across the ionospheric E-region, and not the sharp onset at contact phases that a cavity resonance would produce.
patches.rationale: Dome quotes 'honest caveat' / 'Globe has a competing ionospheric conductivity mechanism' / 'Tier 1 is consistent with both models' cannot be verified from raw-text captures (V51.1 Steelman content). Replace with paraphrase that describes the same admission without unverifiable quotes. Also softens contact-phase claim to correctly distinguish future test (2026 eclipse) from historical ensemble.

patches.find: The Ashour–Chapman conductivity model quantitatively predicts the magnitude, timing, and component-dependence of the magnetic signature when fed with measured total electron content depletion.
patches.replace: The ionospheric conductivity framework of Ashour and Chapman (reviewed in Yamazaki & Maute 2021) predicts the qualitative pattern — Y-component increase, X/Z/F decrease, peak at eclipse maximum, strongest at Sq-vortex latitudes (~30°–50°) — when given measured ionospheric electron content depletion. The specific magnitudes vary with eclipse geometry and ionospheric conditions, but the mechanism correctly predicts sign, component structure, and timing without invoking any dome geometry.
patches.rationale: The Frontiers link is a 2021 review paper, not the original Ashour-Chapman model (Ashour 1950). Text implied the link IS the model. Also: "quantitatively predicts the magnitude" is overstated — magnitudes vary by eclipse. Revised to "predicts the qualitative pattern" which is accurate and harder to attack. Fixes both the mislabeling and the overstatement simultaneously.

patches.find: Kim & Chang 2018 confirmed eclipse geomagnetic effects — standard ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1933)
patches.replace: Triple-counts WIN-010/WIN-025; Chapman Sq mechanism (1933) explains all; dome provides no magnitude derivation
patches.rationale: Finding field should lead with the triple-counting argument — our strongest point. Reader scanning summary table needs to see the duplication before anything else.
WIN-068 was added with detail_group WIN-010/025/068, but WIN-010 still shows WIN-010/025. All three eclipse geomagnetic WINs should reference the full group.
WIN-068 was added with detail_group WIN-010/025/068, but WIN-025 still shows WIN-010/025. All three eclipse geomagnetic WINs should reference the full group.

patches.find: The Nullarbor Highway's ratio of 1.00 (road distance ≈ geodesic distance) reflects the Nullarbor Plain being famously flat and straight — it's a geographic fact, not a dome prediction.
patches.replace: The Nullarbor Plain's road-to-geodesic ratio approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km), though the full Norseman–Ceduna crossing computes closer to ~1.07. Either way, the near-zero excess reflects flat terrain, not dome topology — it's a geographic fact, not a dome prediction.
patches.rationale: Curmudgeon computed Norseman-Ceduna ratio ~1.07, not 1.00. The 1.00 likely refers to the dead-straight central segment. Clarify which segment while preserving the terrain argument.

patches.find: has errors 10,000× larger than standard geodesy
patches.replace: has errors roughly four to five orders of magnitude larger than standard geodesy (10,000× vs. millimeter-level GNSS positioning, up to 250,000× vs. sub-mm VLBI geodesy)
patches.rationale: 10,000× is order-of-magnitude correct but the actual ratio ranges from 6,200× to 248,000×. Adding the range makes the claim more precise and harder to challenge on pedantic grounds.

patches.find: The 'prediction' is circular: dome parameters are set to approximately reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation.
patches.replace:
The 'prediction' is circular: dome parameters are set to approximately reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation.

The strongest dome defense is that standard geodesy also cannot directly predict road distances — Vincenty's formula gives geodesic distances, not road routes. This is true, but terrain-based routing models (using digital elevation and road network data) explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness. The Nullarbor Plain's near-zero road excess occurs precisely where the terrain is flat. If the dome's V13 topology were responsible for the excess, flat terrain should still produce it — topology curves space regardless of terrain. The geographic fact that road excess correlates with terrain roughness, not latitude or topology, is itself evidence that terrain, not topology, drives the pattern. The dome chose road distances as its validation metric; on that metric, standard topographic analysis outperforms dome topology by orders of magnitude.
patches.rationale: Preempts the strongest dome defense (globe also cannot predict roads) by noting terrain-based models do predict road excess, and the Nullarbor flat-terrain case specifically disproves topology-based predictions.
